name: Hermitage of the Virgin of the Eagle and Las Torcas Reservoir Loop
name_original: Ermita de la Virgen del Águila y Embalse de las Torcas — desde Parador Ciclista
description: This difficult 90.7-mile road cycling route from Parador Ciclista explores the Hermitage of the Eagle Virgin and Las Torcas Reservoir.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Cuarte De Huerva traverse a landscape characterized by the Rio Huerva valley, agricultural plains, and gentle hills. The region offers varied terrain, from flat sections along the river to routes with moderate climbs, providing diverse experiences for road cyclists. This area, situated near Zaragoza, features a mix of open countryside and routes passing by local landmarks and reservoirs. Best road cycling routes around Cuarte De Huerva The most popular road cycling route is Virgen de la Dorleta Memorial Loop from Zaragoza, a 40.2 miles (64.7 km) moderate trail that takes 3 hours to complete, featuring a mix of open roads and rolling terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Murales de Alfamén, Parque de Muel y Santuario de la Virgen de las Lagunas — round trip from Zaragoza, a moderate 52.1 miles (83.8 km) path. This route explores the countryside, passing through small towns and offering views of the Muel Park. Local road cyclists also love the Muel Waterfall – View of Cadrete Castle loop from Cuarte de Huerva, a 39.5 miles (63.6 km) trail leading through the Rio Huerva valley and past historical sites, often completed in about 2 hours 42 minutes. Road cycling around Cuarte De Huerva is defined by river valleys, agricultural landscapes, and routes connecting local villages and reservoirs. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels, with more than 60 routes available. The routes in Cuarte De Huerva are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 160 reviews. More than 7,700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Cuarte De Huerva's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Cuarte De Huerva?
A: There are over 60 road cycling routes around Cuarte De Huerva, offering diverse options for all skill levels. This includes 19 easy routes, 36 moderate routes, and 14 difficult routes.

Q: What is the best time of year for road biking in Cuarte De Huerva?
A: The region around Cuarte De Huerva, being close to Zaragoza, generally experiences hot, dry summers and mild winters. Spring (April-May) and Autumn (September-October) are typically ideal for road cycling, offering pleasant temperatures and less extreme weather conditions. Winter can also be suitable for cycling, though some routes might be cooler.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Cuarte De Huerva?
A: Yes, Cuarte De Huerva offers 19 easy road cycling routes. These routes often feature flatter sections along the Rio Huerva valley, providing a gentle introduction to road cycling in the area. You can find options that are less demanding in terms of elevation and distance.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Absolutely. For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, there are 14 difficult road cycling routes around Cuarte De Huerva. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988435650/hermitage-of-the-virgin-of-the-eagle-and-las-torcas-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of the Virgin of the Eagle and Las Torcas Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 145 km with significant elevation gain, offering a demanding ride through varied terrain.

Q: Can I find circular road bike routes in Cuarte De Huerva?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Cuarte De Huerva are designed as loops or round trips, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. For instance, the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992661818/virgen-de-la-dorleta-memorial-loop-from-zaragoza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Virgen de la Dorleta Memorial Loop from Zaragoza&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40000917" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Muel Waterfall – View of Cadrete Castle loop from Cuarte de Huerva&lt;/a&gt; are excellent circular options.

Q: What natural sights can I see along road cycling routes near Cuarte De Huerva?
A: The routes often traverse the Rio Huerva valley, offering views of the river and agricultural landscapes. You might also encounter natural monuments such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1746444" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Three-thorned acacia&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5302773" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ojo de las Almunias&lt;/a&gt;. Some routes also pass by viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6575360" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de las Zorras&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks to visit while road cycling?
A: Yes, the region is rich in history. While cycling, you might pass by significant landmarks such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3007697" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Basilica of Our Lady of the Pillar&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4531361" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cathedral of the Savior (La Seo of Zaragoza)&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40000917" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Muel Waterfall – View of Cadrete Castle loop from Cuarte de Huerva&lt;/a&gt;, for example, leads past historical sites including views of Cadrete Castle.

Q: What is the typical duration for road cycling routes in Cuarte De Huerva?
A: The duration varies significantly depending on the route's length and difficulty. Many moderate routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992661818/virgen-de-la-dorleta-memorial-loop-from-zaragoza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Virgen de la Dorleta Memorial Loop from Zaragoza&lt;/a&gt; (64.7 km), typically take around 3 hours. Longer, more difficult routes can extend to 5-7 hours or more, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e988435650/hermitage-of-the-virgin-of-the-eagle-and-las-torcas-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of the Virgin of the Eagle and Las Torcas Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; which takes over 7 hours.

Q: Are there road bike routes in Cuarte De Huerva with parking facilities?
A: While specific parking details for every route are not always listed, many routes around Cuarte De Huerva start from towns or villages where public parking is generally available. If starting from Zaragoza, there are various parking options in and around the city from which you can begin your ride.

Q: Can I find places to stop for refreshments or a cafe along the routes?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes in the Cuarte De Huerva area pass through or near small towns and villages where you can find cafes, bars, or shops for refreshments. Routes that explore the countryside, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989987141/murales-de-alfamen-parque-de-muel-y-santuario-de-la-virgen-de-las-lagunas-round-trip-from-zaragoza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Murales de Alfamén, Parque de Muel y Santuario de la Virgen de las Lagunas — round trip from Zaragoza&lt;/a&gt;, often offer opportunities to stop in local communities.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Cuarte De Huerva?
A: The road cycling routes in Cuarte De Huerva are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 160 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from river valleys to gentle hills, and the scenic views of the agricultural plains and local landmarks. The mix of open roads and routes connecting charming villages is also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there less crowded road cycling options around Cuarte De Huerva?
A: While popular routes can see more traffic, the extensive network of over 60 routes means you can often find quieter roads, especially those venturing further into the agricultural plains or less-trafficked rural areas. Exploring routes that don't pass through major towns can offer a more serene cycling experience.
tips: At the top of Pico del Águila is the Hermitage of the Virgen del Águila. The site is in perfect condition thanks to the excellent work of the residents of the town of Paniza. In summer it is usually open and you can visit it. The views are magnificent of the Sierra de Algairén, behind that of Moncayo. It has a table at the back sheltered from the wind and in full sun, which is perfect for recovering energy and/or temperature in winter.

Tosos is a small town located on the banks of the Huerva river. The alluvial terraces formed by the passage of the river have created natural differences where the inhabitants of the area take advantage of this by planting fruit trees typical of these dry spaces.
La Casaza, is a fortified watchtower, located on the Torcas Reservoir, in the Zaragoza municipality of Tosos, 2 km from the town and a few kilometers from the Torcas reservoir.
Near the town of Tosos and Aguilón we find ourselves in the dam with wonderful views of the Torcas Reservoir that is part of the Huerva River.

faqs.question: How challenging is the road cycling route "Hermitage of the Eagle Virgin and Las Torcas Reservoir — from Parador Ciclista"?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as difficult, featuring significant elevation gain of approximately 1,549 meters over its 146 km distance. Cyclists can expect varied terrain, including rolling hills and challenging climbs, requiring a very good fitness level. The route primarily uses paved, quiet country lanes, though some sections near remote landmarks might include unpaved surfaces less suitable for road bikes.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain and scenery can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The route offers diverse and picturesque scenery. You'll cycle through varied terrain, from flatlands to rolling hills and challenging ascents. The landscapes often feature olive and fruit tree terraces, rivers, and expansive reservoir vistas, particularly around Torcas Reservoir. Culturally, you may encounter historical sites and ancient towns, with the Hermitage of the Virgin of the Eagle being a key landmark.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: As the route starts "from Parador Ciclista," it implies beginning near a Parador that caters to cyclists. These establishments typically offer secure parking facilities for guests. It is advisable to confirm parking availability and any associated costs directly with the specific Parador you plan to use as your starting point.

faqs.question: Is the route suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: Given its difficult classification, significant distance (146 km), and considerable elevation gain (1,549 m), this specific route is not recommended for beginners or families with young children. It requires a high level of fitness and experience in road cycling. However, regions associated with Paradores often offer easier, more leisurely cycling options nearby for different fitness levels.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: Key highlights along this route include the historic Hermitage of the Virgin of the Eagle, the charming settlement of Tosos, the ancient La Casaza Castle, and the scenic Torcas Reservoir. These offer opportunities for cultural immersion and breathtaking natural views.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route generally depends on local weather patterns. To avoid extreme heat, spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) are often ideal, offering pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery. Summer can be very hot, making early morning starts essential, while winter might bring colder temperatures and potentially adverse conditions, especially at higher elevations.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, primarily on paved surfaces. While there are no specific restrictions against dogs on public roads, it's generally not advisable to bring dogs on long, difficult road cycling tours due to safety concerns for both the dog and the cyclist, and the lack of suitable amenities. If you plan to stop at any establishments, check their pet policies beforehand.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are generally required to cycle on public roads or access the natural areas along this route. However, if you plan to visit specific attractions like the Hermitage of the Virgin of the Eagle or La Casaza Castle, there might be separate entrance fees or specific visiting hours.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with several local paths. You will encounter very small sections of official trails such as PR-Z 046, PR-Z 55, and Sendas Santa Fe. These intersections are minimal, with each covering less than 2% of the overall route.

faqs.question: What amenities can I expect if starting from a Parador Ciclista?
faqs.answer: Paradores Ciclistas are specifically designed to cater to cyclists. If you start from one, you can typically expect amenities such as secure bike storage, workshops for minor repairs, and bike washing areas. Some Paradores even allow you to bring your bike into your room, making them an ideal base for cycling adventures.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or find accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through settlements like Tosos, where you may find local cafes, restaurants, or small accommodations. Additionally, starting from a Parador Ciclista provides a comfortable base with dining and lodging options. It's always a good idea to research specific establishments and their opening hours in advance, especially in smaller villages.
